title: Confidence uncertainty: position and momentum can be jointly determined with a guaranteed probability
authors: Jia-Yi Lin, Xin-Yu Li, Wei Wang, Shengjun Wu
abstract: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that the position and momentum of a particle cannot be sharply determined simultaneously. Standard-deviation and entropic formulations capture the spread of the probability distribution but say little about the probability itself contained in a small region. We introduce the "confidence uncertainty" $Δ^{c}x(θ_x)$ as the minimal Lebesgue measure of the support set in which the particle is found with probability at least $θ_x$, and the companion "interval confidence uncertainty" $Δ^{I}x(θ_x)$ which restricts the support to a single interval. We prove two complementary uncertainty inequalities. (i) For $θ_x+θ_p\le 1$ both confidence uncertainties can be made arbitrarily small simultaneously, so that no nontrivial product bound holds; in particular, position and momentum can be jointly localised with probability at least~$50\%$. (ii) For $θ_x+θ_p>1$ a lower bound holds: combining Lenard's projection inequality with the Donoho--Stark operator-norm bound we obtain $Δ^{c}x\,Δ^{c}p\geq 2π\hbar\bigl(\sqrt{θ_xθ_p}-\sqrt{(1-θ_x)(1-θ_p)}\bigr)^{\!2}$, and for the interval version we obtain the sharp implicit Landau--Pollak bound $Δ^{I}x\,Δ^{I}p\geq 4\hbar\,λ_{0}^{-1}\!\bigl((\sqrt{θ_xθ_p}-\sqrt{(1-θ_x)(1-θ_p)})^{2}\bigr)$, where $λ_{0}(c)$ is the largest prolate-spheroidal eigenvalue. We support the analytical bounds with numerical evaluation of $λ_{0}(c)$, provide closed-form small-$c$ and large-$c$ asymptotics, compute the optimal Slepian-superposition states that saturate the interval bound, and compare the resulting product against the variance Heisenberg--Kennard, the Białynicki-Birula--Mycielski entropic, and the Donoho--Stark concentration bounds.
summary: The paper introduces a new way to measure uncertainty based on the probability of finding a particle within specific intervals rather than just the spread of the distribution. It reveals that while position and momentum can be jointly localized to arbitrary precision if the total confidence is low, a strict limit emerges when the required confidence levels are high. This provides a tighter alternative to the traditional Heisenberg uncertainty principle for specific probability-based applications.
main_problem: Addressing the limitations of standard-deviation and entropic uncertainty principles by quantifying the probability of finding a particle within specific small regions of position and momentum.
main_result: The authors introduce 'confidence uncertainty' and prove a two-regime phase diagram: for confidence levels where theta_x + theta_p <= 1, position and momentum can be jointly localized arbitrarily well, whereas for theta_x + theta_p > 1, a strict positive lower bound applies.
method: The study utilizes Lenard's projection inequality, the Donoho-Stark operator-norm bound, and Slepian-Pollak-Landau theory involving prolate-spheroidal wave functions.
model_or_system: A quantum particle in a pure state described by position (x) and momentum (p) representations.
key_observables: Confidence uncertainty (minimal Lebesgue measure of the support set), interval confidence uncertainty, and the probability mass (theta_x, theta_p) within specific regions.
important_parameters: Confidence levels (theta_x, theta_p), the time-bandwidth product (c), and the transition boundary defined by theta_x + theta_p = 1.
main_assumptions: The primary results focus on pure quantum states, though the framework can be extended to mixed states.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a phase diagram of the (theta_x, theta_p) plane; Figure 2 displays the largest Slepian eigenvalue on linear and log scales; Figure 3 compares the new Landau-Pollak bound against the elementary bound.
paper_structure: The paper introduces new uncertainty measures, establishes a phase diagram for two distinct regimes, provides analytical proofs for lower bounds using signal processing theory, performs numerical evaluations of Slepian eigenvalues, and compares the new bounds against established Heisenberg, entropic, and Donoho-Stark bounds.
why_interesting: This work provides a more operational way to understand joint localization in phase space, which is highly relevant for quantum optics and precision measurements where the probability of finding a particle in a specific window is more critical than the variance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 3 compares the Landau–Pollak bound of Theo- rem 2 against the elementary bound (16). Both bounds agree on θx = 1, but the new one extends to all θx+θp > 1 and is consistently strictly tighter on the diagonal. Ta- ble II samples the tight bound across the (θx, θp)-plane; Fig. 4 displays the resulting landscape.

FIG. 4. Numerical landscape of the tight bound on ∆Ix ∆Ip/ℏover (θx, θp) ∈[0, 1]2. The bound diverges as both confidences approach unity; the dashed line θx + θp = 1 separates the trivial region (Theorem 1).
